Comparative analysis of conventional and IRMS techniques for honey adulteration detection in accordance with ISIRI standards
============================================================================================================================

[_BMC Research Notes_](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/)**volume 18**, Article number:226 (2025) [Cite this article](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#citeas)

*   15 Accesses

*   1 Altmetric

*   [Metrics details](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z/metrics)

Abstract
--------

### Objective

This study assesses the comparative performance of Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometry (IRMS) and conventional techniques in detecting honey adulteration. Standard analytical methods, in accordance with ISIRI guidelines, were evaluated alongside δ 13 C isotopic analysis via IRMS across a dataset of 20 honey samples. Conventional analytical techniques were employed to evaluate parameters such as pre-hydrolysis reducing sugars, sucrose content, the fructose-to-glucose ratio, proline, and hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF). The advanced IRMS technique was utilized to determine δ 13 C values for glucose with high accuracy and sensitivity. The carbohydrate profile of honey, including glucose, fructose, disaccharides, and trisaccharides, was analyzed using LC/IRMS. A comparative assessment of both methods was conducted on twenty honey samples, following ISIRI guidelines.

### Results

While conventional methods met ISIRI criteria in 18 out of 20 samples, IRMS produced contrasting findings—only 2 samples were identified as genuine and free from adulteration. Statistical evaluation using Prism software revealed a significant discrepancy between the two techniques, with a p-value < 0.05, indicating stronger confidence in IRMS results. The findings highlight the necessity of reconsidering current protocols for honey adulteration detection in order to preserve the purity and credibility of this high-value natural product.

### Graphical Abstract

![Image 4](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs13104-025-07287-z/MediaObjects/13104_2025_7287_Figa_HTML.png)

[Peer Review reports](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z/peer-review)

Introduction
------------

Honey, valued for its natural composition, is increasingly susceptible to sophisticated adulteration. Traditional detection methods, though widely adopted, often fail to uncover fraud. This study integrates IRMS, a high-precision isotopic technique, with conventional methods, specifically within ISIRI standards, to evaluate authenticity. The goal is to validate IRMS as a superior alternative and to propose its broader applicability in food fraud detection.

Honey is one of the sweet and tasty foods produced naturally by bees. [[1](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR1 "Banti M. Food adulteration and some methods of detection, review. Int J Nutr Food Sci. 2020;9(3):86."),[2](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR2 "Girma Tura A, Bersissa SD. ALP Sci. 2008;520:63–4."),[9](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR9 "Davis SC, Simpson J, Gil-Vega KDC, Niechayev NA, Van Tongerlo EV, Castano NH, et al. Undervalued potential of crassulacean acid metabolism for current and future agricultural production. J Exp Bot. 2019;70(22):6521–37.")][[10](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR10 "Mehryar L, Esmaiili M. Honey & Honey Adulteration Detection : A Review. Int Congr Eng Food 11th [Internet]. 2011;1–6. Available from: 
                  http://www.icef11.org/content/papers/nfp/nfp1066.pdf
                  
                ")]. This product always plays a unique role in the household market, and its daily consumption is recommended for providing energy. According to the EU definition, honey is “the natural sweet substance produced by _Apis melliferabees_ from the nectar of plants or from secretions of living parts of plants or excretions of plant-sucking insects or the living parts of plants, which the bees collect, transform by combining with specific substances of their own, deposit, dehydrate, store, and leave in honeycombs to ripen and mature,” therefore based on EU honey definition, it is a perfectly natural products, it must not be produced artificially or synthetically.[[10](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR10 "Mehryar L, Esmaiili M. Honey & Honey Adulteration Detection : A Review. Int Congr Eng Food 11th [Internet]. 2011;1–6. Available from: 
                  http://www.icef11.org/content/papers/nfp/nfp1066.pdf
                  
                "),[11](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR11 "EUR-Lex - l21124a - EN - EUR-Lex 
                  https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=LEGISSUM%3Al21124a
                  
                . Accessd 25 May 2021"),[12](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR12 "Fakhlaei R, Selamat J, Khatib A, Razi AFA, Sukor R, Ahmad S, et al. The toxic impact of honey adulteration: a review. Foods. 2020;9(11):1–21."),[13](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR13 "Yadata D. Detection of the electrical conductivity and acidity of honey from different areas of Tepi. Food Sci Technol. 2014;2(5):59–63."),[14](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR14 "Dong H, Luo D, Xian Y, Luo H, Guo X, Li C, et al. Adulteration identification of commercial honey with the C-4 sugar content of negative values by an elemental analyzer and liquid chromatography coupled to isotope ratio mass spectroscopy. J Agric Food Chem. 2016;64(16):3258–65.")]. However, honey is produced in special seasons when the weather becomes warmer. Adulterated honey is much cheaper than natural one, and also, there are represented everywhere. [[15](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR15 "Aliaño-González MJ, Ferreiro-González M, Espada-Bellido E, Palma M, Barbero GF. A screening method based on headspace-ion mobility spectrometry to identify adulterated honey. Sensors. 2019. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/s19071621
                  
                ."),[16](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR16 "FAO, WHO. Food adulteration: its challenges and impacts. Food Sci Qual Manag. 2007;2015(41):50–6."),[17](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR17 "Dinca OR, Ionete RE, Popescu R, Costinel D, Radu GL. Geographical and botanical origin discrimination of Romanian honey using complex stable isotope data and Chemometrics. Food Anal Methods. 2015;8(2):401–12."),[18](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR18 "Naila A, Flint SH, Sulaiman AZ, Ajit A, Weeds Z. Classical and novel approaches to the analysis of honey and detection of adulterants. Food Control. 2018;90:152–65. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodcont.2018.02.027
                  
                .")].

Laboratory conventional methods included sugar determination before and after hydrolysis, diastasis activity, Proline contents, Etc. The procedures are mentioned in Iranian National Standard (ISIRI) No. 92. However, these routine methods could not have identified adulteration in a wide range.

One of the new methods used to determine the authenticity of honey approved by the European Union is the study of stable carbon isotopes by mass spectrometry. This method examines the ratio of carbon 13 to carbon 12 [[18](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR18 "Naila A, Flint SH, Sulaiman AZ, Ajit A, Weeds Z. Classical and novel approaches to the analysis of honey and detection of adulterants. Food Control. 2018;90:152–65. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodcont.2018.02.027
                  
                ."), [22](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR22 "Kirschbaum J, Krause C, Brückner H. Liquid chromatographic quantification of synthetic colorants in fish roe and caviar. Eur Food Res Technol. 2006;222(5–6):572–9.")].

In hydrocarbon production, plants are divided into three main categories: C3, C4, and CAM (Crassulacean acid metabolism) [[18](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR18 "Naila A, Flint SH, Sulaiman AZ, Ajit A, Weeds Z. Classical and novel approaches to the analysis of honey and detection of adulterants. Food Control. 2018;90:152–65. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodcont.2018.02.027
                  
                ."), [22](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR22 "Kirschbaum J, Krause C, Brückner H. Liquid chromatographic quantification of synthetic colorants in fish roe and caviar. Eur Food Res Technol. 2006;222(5–6):572–9.")].

Since C4 plants absorb and stabilize more carbon dioxide from the atmosphere than C3 plants, the ratio of carbon 13 to carbon 12 of these plants is higher than that of C3 plants. This value in C4 plants is approximately −8 to −16, but this number in C3 plants is equivalent to -22 to -32 ‰. Bees usually feed on flowering plants that belong to the C3 group [[4](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR4 "Song X, She S, Xin M, Chen L, Li Y, Vander HY, et al. Detection of adulteration in Chinese monofloral honey using 1H nuclear magnetic resonance and chemometrics. J Food Compos Anal. 2020;86:103390."), [8](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR8 "Bogdanov S, Gallman P. Authenticity of honey and other bee products State of the art Technical-scientific information. ALP Sci. 2008;520:63–4."), [10](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR10 "Mehryar L, Esmaiili M. Honey & Honey Adulteration Detection : A Review. Int Congr Eng Food 11th [Internet]. 2011;1–6. Available from: 
                  http://www.icef11.org/content/papers/nfp/nfp1066.pdf
                  
                "), [12](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR12 "Fakhlaei R, Selamat J, Khatib A, Razi AFA, Sukor R, Ahmad S, et al. The toxic impact of honey adulteration: a review. Foods. 2020;9(11):1–21."),[13](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR13 "Yadata D. Detection of the electrical conductivity and acidity of honey from different areas of Tepi. Food Sci Technol. 2014;2(5):59–63."),[14](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR14 "Dong H, Luo D, Xian Y, Luo H, Guo X, Li C, et al. Adulteration identification of commercial honey with the C-4 sugar content of negative values by an elemental analyzer and liquid chromatography coupled to isotope ratio mass spectroscopy. J Agric Food Chem. 2016;64(16):3258–65."), [20](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR20 "Li Y, Huang Y, Xia J, Xiong Y, Min S. Quantitative analysis of honey adulteration by spectrum analysis combined with several high-level data fusion strategies. Vib Spectrosc. 2020;108:103060."), [21](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR21 "Sotiropoulou NS, Xagoraris M, Revelou PK, Kaparakou E, Kanakis C, Pappas C, et al. The use of spme-gc-ms ir and raman techniques for botanical and geographical authentication and detection of adulteration of honey. Foods. 2021;10(7):3."), [23](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR23 "Moldovan D, Pop M, Fechete R, Baudouine A, Todica M. Investigation of the effects of degradation of high density polyethylene by 13C NMR spectroscopy and 1H relaxometry. Stud Univ Babes-Bolyai Chem. 2011;3:103–10."),[24](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR24 "Lutz E, Kurt-peter R. Original article Improved detection of honey adulteration by measuring differences between 13 C / 12 C stable carbon isotope ratios of protein and sugar compounds with a combination of elemental analyzer - isotope ratio mass spectrometry and liquid chroma. Apidologie. 2008;39:574–87."),[25](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR25 "Subari N, Saleh JM, Shakaff AYM, Zakaria A. A hybrid sensing approach for pure and adulterated honey classification. Sensors (Switzerland). 2012;12(10):14022–40.")]. Therefore, the ratio of carbon 13 isotope to carbon 12 in honey produced is approximately equal to the same ratio in C3 plants. As a result, if counterfeiting is done using other plants, including C4 plants, this can be determined by examining changes in the isotopic ratio [[26](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR26 "Kropf U, Golob T, Nečemer M, Kump P, Korošec M, Bertoncelj J, et al. Carbon and nitrogen natural stable isotopes in slovene honey: Adulteration and botanical and geographical aspects. J Agric Food Chem. 2010;58(24):12794–803."),[27](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR27 "Ciursa P, Oroian M. Voltammetric e-tongue for honey adulteration detection. Sensors. 2021. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/s21155059
                  
                ."),[28](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR28 "Ferrari RC, Bittencourt PP, Rodrigues MA, Moreno-Villena JJ, Alves FRR, Gastaldi VD, et al. C4 and crassulacean acid metabolism within a single leaf: deciphering key components behind a rare photosynthetic adaptation. New Phytol. 2020;225(4):1699–714.")].

Nowadays, methods of cheating in honey, due to the awareness of fraudsters in various fields, have advanced a lot, and diagnostic methods should be more accurate according to these conditions and be able to detect fraud even in small quantities. Therefore, using the IRMS device for high accuracy in measuring the ratio of carbon isotopes is an excellent option to detect fraud [[4](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR4 "Song X, She S, Xin M, Chen L, Li Y, Vander HY, et al. Detection of adulteration in Chinese monofloral honey using 1H nuclear magnetic resonance and chemometrics. J Food Compos Anal. 2020;86:103390."), [18](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR18 "Naila A, Flint SH, Sulaiman AZ, Ajit A, Weeds Z. Classical and novel approaches to the analysis of honey and detection of adulterants. Food Control. 2018;90:152–65. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodcont.2018.02.027
                  
                ."), [23](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR23 "Moldovan D, Pop M, Fechete R, Baudouine A, Todica M. Investigation of the effects of degradation of high density polyethylene by 13C NMR spectroscopy and 1H relaxometry. Stud Univ Babes-Bolyai Chem. 2011;3:103–10."), [29](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR29 "Kerkvliet JD, Meijer HAJ. Adulteration of honey: Relation between microscopic analysis and δ13C measurements. Apidologie. 2000;31(6):717–26.")].

Due to fraud detection using IRMS, this instrument is coupled with high-performance liquid chromatography. One of the advantages of using high-performance liquid chromatography is the study of a wide range of exogenous carbon and does not require long extraction steps. In addition, using this method is cost-effective and reduces sample analysis time [[2](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR2 "Girma Tura A, Bersissa SD. Review on honey adulteration and detection of adulterants in honey. Int J Gastroenterol. 2020;4(1):1."), [30](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR30 "Mai Z, Lai B, Sun M, Shao J, Guo L. Food adulteration and traceability tests using stable carbon isotope technologies. Trop J Pharm Res. 2019;18(8):1771–84."),[31](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR31 "Salvador L, Guijarro M, Rubio D, Aucatoma B, Guillén T, Jentzsch PV, et al. Exploratory monitoring of the quality and authenticity of commercial honey in Ecuador. Foods. 2019;8(3):1–13."),[32](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR32 "Nisbet C, Kazak F, Ardalı Y. Determination of quality criteria that allow differentiation between honey adulterated with sugar and pure honey. Biol Trace Elem Res. 2018;186(1):288–93."),[33](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#ref-CR33 "Bogdanov S, Gallman P. Authenticity of honey and other bee products State of the art Technical-scientific information. ALP Sci. 2008;520:63–4.")].

The purpose of this study is the determination of the carbon isotope ratio and honey proteins in the Iran market and evaluate by routine laboratory methods based on the Iranian National Standard Organization. In this study, researchers exposed a variety of honey adulteration in the Iran market; therefore, they have compared the routine methods with novel methods. Since this study has yet to be conducted in Iran so far, this study is significant in terms of data collection in monitoring food security in Iran. This research can also prove the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of routine honey monitoring methods, cause significant progress in producing quality and original honey and improve honey analysis methods.

Material and method
-------------------

### Reagents and standards

Reagents included glucose, fructose, and sucrose standards (Sigma Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) with purities > 99%. Analytical-grade chemicals (Merck, Germany) were used alongside deionized water purified using MiliQ 8 systems (Australia). Equipment comprised an EA-IRMS system calibrated with IAEA-600 standards for δ13C values and a HiPlex-Ca column for LC-IRMS analysis.

### Sample collection and preparation

Twenty honey samples were sourced from local beekeepers and markets across various Iranian regions (April 2021–January 2022). Samples were stored in amber glass containers at room temperature. Preparation involved protein extraction using sodium tungstate and sulfuric acid, followed by analysis with IRMS and conventional ISIRI protocols.

### Analytical techniques

Conventional Methods: Analysis of reducing sugars, sucrose content, proline, and hydroxymethylfurfural per ISIRI No. 92 standards.

IRMS Analysis: δ13C values for glucose, fructose, and proteins were determined using EA-IRMS and LC-IRMS, calibrated against international standards.

### EA-IRMS analysis

We have employed an Elemental Analyzer (EA) system equipped with combustion and reduction furnaces to determine the isotopic composition of honey, including bulk carbon isotope ratios, mass spectrometric analysis, and the δ 13 C values of isolated protein fractions. To ensure measurement accuracy, the IRMS instrument was calibrated using a single-point normalization protocol, based on the IAEA-600 standard (caffeine, VPDB = –27.771‰ for δ 13 C).

To date, we have analyzed honey samples along with their extracted proteins and individual sugar components—sucrose, glucose, and fructose—using the EA-IRMS technique. Protein extraction from the carbohydrate matrix of honey was carried out by mixing 12 g of each honey sample with 4 mL of deionized water, followed by the addition of 2 mL of 10% sodium tungstate and 2 mL of 0.33 M sulfuric acid. The mixture was heated to temperatures exceeding 80°C and then centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 5 min. The resulting protein pellet was washed five times with deionized water and subsequently dried in an oven at 70°C for 3 h.

### LC-IRMS analysis

The researcher sought to describe our LC-IRMS protocol, which utilizes a calcium-based cation exchange column (HiPlex-Ca). This chromatographic column is maintained at 85°C, and 10 μL of either the sample or standard solution is injected. Following carbohydrate separation, the eluent is directed into an acidification medium (LiquiF) and subsequently oxidized. Carbon is then converted into CO₂ within the combustion interface, and the resulting CO₂ passes through a gas-permeable membrane into a helium carrier stream, which delivers the gas to the IRMS for δ 13 C/δ 12 C isotope ratio determination.

Deionized water was used as the mobile phase, flowing through the chromatography column at 0.1 mL/min. Mixtures of glucose, sucrose, and fructose were prepared at 12.322 mg/L, 16.477 mg/L, and 12.301 mg/L, respectively, in HPLC-grade water. Isotope ratios of each sugar component were independently analyzed using EA-IRMS, calibrated against the IAEA-600 international standard (caffeine; VPDB δ 13 C = –27.771‰).

Our analysis demonstrated that the R value (12 CO₂/13 CO₂) varied across samples and standards. However, most changes in isotope ratios were minimal, typically reported in percent (%), per mil (‰), or parts per million (ppm). The chromatographic profile revealed distinct peaks: the first peak corresponded to trisaccharides, the second to disaccharides, the third to glucose, and the final peak to fructose. A reference table for quantitative δ 13 C values across various honey types is presented in Table[1](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#Tab1).

**Table 1 Proposed limits for the Δδ 13 C values as purity criteria for honey**

[Full size table](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z/tables/1)

### Iranian conventional method analysis

The conventional analytical framework was based on ISIRI Standard No. 92, which outlines a diverse set of procedures summarized in Table[2](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z#Tab2). Six authentication indices were applied for the detection of honey adulteration. Among these, the most reliable and validated parameters were selected for this study, while the less applicable methods were excluded.

**Table 2 Honey authentication properties**

[Full size table](https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13104-025-07287-z/tables/2)

### Statistical analysis

To ensure the validity and reliability of the comparative results, comprehensive statistical tests were conducted using GraphPad Prism 9.3.1. A chi-square test was performed to evaluate the statistical significance of differences between the outcomes of conventional methods and the advanced IRMS technique.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometry (IRMS) offers enhanced accuracy over conventional techniques, particularly in detecting honey adulteration through carbon isotopic ratios.
IRMS exhibits superior sensitivity to identify subtle adulteration, distinguishing between authentic honey and blended sugars from C4 plants, which conventional methods may overlook.
Conventional methods often fail to detect diverse forms of honey adulteration, relying on chemical parameters that can be misleading, as highlighted in recent comparative studies.
IRMS provides high precision in food safety applications, significantly reducing false positives in adulteration detection, thereby ensuring the integrity of honey products.
Case studies reveal a stark contrast in findings; IRMS identified significant adulteration in honey samples, while conventional methods falsely classified many as authentic.
